title: On the Quantum Unique Ergodicity Conjecture for Hyperbolic Arithmetic Manifolds
speaker: Zvi Shem-Tov
abstract: We will discuss recent results towards the quantum unique ergodicity conjecture of Rudnick and Sarnak, concerning the distribution of Hecke--Maass forms on hyperbolic arithmetic manifolds. The conjecture was resolved for congruence surfaces by Lindenstrauss and we focus on manifolds of higher dimension, where substantial new difficulties arise. Based on joint works with Alexandre de Faveri and Lior Silberman.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Quantum limits and the QUE question
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Laplacian eigenfunction probability measures, recalls quantum ergodicity for ergodic classical dynamics, formulates quantum unique ergodicity as uniqueness of the limiting measure, and identifies hyperbolic manifolds as the strongly chaotic setting of interest.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic quotients and Hecke symmetry
proof_stages.summary: For congruence arithmetic hyperbolic manifolds, the speaker introduces Hecke correspondences as finite averaging operators commuting with the Laplacian and reformulates them through p-adic symmetric spaces and Hecke spheres.

proof_stages.label: Known surface theorem, higher-dimensional obstruction, and main results
proof_stages.summary: Lindenstrauss’s arithmetic QUE theorem for surfaces is contrasted with the higher-dimensional problem, where intermediate subgroups and totally geodesic submanifolds create new obstructions; the stated results prove QUE for the three-dimensional/product cases and constrain four-dimensional quantum limits to convex combinations of volume and three-dimensional homogeneous pieces, with no escape of mass.

proof_stages.label: Microlocal lift to an invariant measure problem
proof_stages.summary: Quantum limits are lifted from the base manifold to the homogeneous space or frame-bundle level, where the lifted measures are invariant under the one-parameter geodesic-flow subgroup and still arise from Hecke eigenfunctions.

proof_stages.label: Measure rigidity reduction to homogeneous measures
proof_stages.summary: A Lindenstrauss-style measure-classification principle says that recurrence, positive entropy, and non-exceptional-return properties force the lifted A-invariant limits to be convex combinations of homogeneous measures, but in higher dimensions there are many such homogeneous competitors.

proof_stages.label: Bad homogeneous supports and Hecke amplification
proof_stages.summary: Candidate homogeneous supports are contained in sets of the form H0M that are generally not subgroup orbits, so the proof seeks to show their measure is zero by Hecke amplification, bounding mass of epsilon-neighborhoods via intersection counts of Hecke translates divided by large Hecke eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Weak smallness from p-adic counting and sup-norm methods
proof_stages.summary: The amplification counts are related to Marshall-type sup-norm estimates, leading to a weak-smallness/temperedness condition on rational stabilizers expressed through p-adic sphere growth, which yields polynomial decay of tubes and hence positive entropy when the relevant centralizers are weakly small.

proof_stages.label: Parallel-return induction and remaining non-small obstructions
proof_stages.summary: For non-group supports one counts only parallel returns and handles transverse intersections by induction on dimension, giving a criterion for zero mass in terms of weakly small stabilizers while explaining the residual higher-dimensional obstructions through non-small subgroups and lifts.

examples.name: Disk versus Bunimovich stadium billiards
examples.note: The contrast between structured disk eigenfunction densities and apparently uniform stadium densities is used to draw the guiding link from quantum limits to the ergodicity of the corresponding billiard flow.
examples.engagement_reason: the talk sets up the Laplace eigenfunction measures for the disk and stadium, compares their pictures, and reasons via billiard trajectories and ergodicity to explain the contrast

examples.name: Hassell bouncing-ball modes in the stadium
examples.note: The stadium’s bouncing-ball subsequences illustrate that ordinary ergodicity does not force every eigenfunction to equidistribute, motivating the restriction to more strongly chaotic hyperbolic settings.
examples.engagement_reason: Hassell’s bouncing-ball modes are cited as known counterexamples/subsequences concentrating in the stadium, but no construction or calculation is carried out

passes.reason: Hassell’s modes concentrate on the marginal bouncing-ball family and refute replacing “most” eigenfunctions by “all.”

Hassell’s bouncing-ball modes are localized/collapsed onto a marginal bouncing-ball family and are invoked to show that full QUE fails for general ergodic billiards.
Hassell’s bouncing-ball modes are deployed as the failure of QUE for general ergodic billiards, while not being a sporadic, extremal, or maximally symmetric object.
Hassell’s bouncing-ball subsequence uses a marginal non-isolated periodic family in the stadium to disprove full equidistribution for general ergodic billiards.
Hassell’s modes localize on the marginal bouncing-ball family and refute QUE for general ergodic billiards.
Hassell's bouncing-ball subsequence is cited to refute removing 'most' from quantum ergodicity, relying on a marginal one-parameter family of nonhyperbolic stadium orbits.
Hassell’s modes refute all-eigenfunction equidistribution for ergodic billiards by exploiting the stadium’s marginal bouncing-ball family.

examples.name: Modular surface and the classical Hecke correspondence
